coredata.dc:title: Thermal equilibrium characteristic of large-size lithium-ion pouch battery: resting time between charge and discharge
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yang
Chen, Mingbiao
Bai, Fanfei
Song, Wenji
Feng, Ziping
coredata.dc:description: The temperature rise of lithium-ion battery discharging varies with current and ambient temperature. As a key factor to ensure the repeatability of battery experiments, the time of battery reaches thermal equilibrium after discharging will be figured out in this work by experimental and theoretical analysis. The errors of experimental apparatus was confirmed by an innovatively statistical analysis. The pouch battery being divided to 7 areas, convection heat transfer coefficient of each area was gotten with lumped parameter method. The experimental results also verified reasonability of lumped parameter method and theoretical analysis in this question. The cooling time could be precisely predicted by theoretical analysis. What’s more, the whole battery could be regarded as thermal equilibrium while the variation of battery surface temperature is small enough. This work provides a theoretical basis of battery thermal equilibrium to experiments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: lithium-ion battery
thermal equilibrium
heat conduction differential equation
lumped parameter method
statistical analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids confeditor contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj suppl tomb vol volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2623 2630 1876-6102 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of ICAE2018 â€“ The 10th International Conference on Applied Energy. 10.1016/j.egypro.2019.02.013 10.1016/j.egypro.2019.02.013 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of ICAE2018 â€“ The 10th International Conference on Applied Energy. 1876-6102 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018). 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018), 22-25 August 2018, Hong Kong, China Thermal equilibrium characteristic of large-size lithium-ion pouch battery: resti g time between charge and discharge Yang Lia,b,c,d, Mingbiao Chena,c,d, Fanfei Baia,b,c,d, Wenji Songa,c,d,*, Ziping Fenga,c,d aGuangzhou Institute of Energy Conversion, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Guangzhou, 510640, China bUniversity of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100000, China cCAS Key Laboratory of Renewable Energy, Guangzhou, 510640, China dGuangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New and Renewable Energy Research and Development, Guangzhou 510640, China Abstract The temperature rise of lithium-ion battery discharging varies with current and ambient temperature. As a key factor to ensure the repeatability of battery experiments, the time of battery reaches thermal equilibrium after discharging will be figured out in this work by experimental and theoretical analysis. The errors of experimental apparatus was confirmed by an innovatively statistical analysis. The pouch battery being divided to 7 areas, convection heat transfer coefficient of each area was gotten with lumped parameter method. The expe imental results also verified r ason bility of lumped parameter method and theoretical an lysis in this question. Th cooling time could be precisely predicted by theoret cal analysis. Wha â€™s more, the whol battery could be regarded as hermal equilibrium while the variation of battery surface temperature is small enoug . This work provides a the retical basis of battery th rmal equilibrium to experiments. C pyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Select on and pee -review under esp sibil ty of the scientific committee of the 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018). Keywords: lithium-ion battery; thermal equilibrium; eat conduction iff rential equation; lumped parameter method; statistical analysis. 1. Introduction Lithium ion batteries ar widely us d in the fields of renewable e ergy and traditional energy, because of the hig energy density[1]. Currently, the performance of commercially available lithium-ion batteries, including true capability, rate capability, calendar life, cycle life and cycle efficiency, are sensitivity to the temperature. For * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86 - 020 - 87059478; fax: +86 - 020 - 87057795. E-mail address: songwj@ms.giec.ac.cn Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Co yright Â© 2018 Els vier Ltd. All rig ts reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018). 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018), 22-25 August 2018, Hong Kong, China Thermal equilibrium characteristic of large-size lithium-ion pouch battery: resting time between charge and discharge Yang Lia,b,c,d, Mingbiao Chena,c,d, Fanfei Baia,b,c,d, Wenji Songa,c,d,*, Ziping Fenga,c,d aGuangzhou Institute of Energy o version, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Guangzhou, 510640, China bUniv rsity f Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100000, China cCAS Key Laboratory of l , Guangz ou, 510640, Chi a dGuangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New and Renewable Energy Research and Development, Guangzhou 510640, China Abstract The temperature rise of lithium-ion battery discharging varies with current and ambient temperature. As a key factor to ensure the repeatability of battery experiments, the time of battery reaches thermal equilibrium after discharging will be figured out in this work by experimental and theoretical analysis. The errors of experimental apparatus was confirmed by an innovatively statistical analysis. The pouch battery being divided to 7 areas, convection heat transfer coefficient of each area was gotten with lumped parameter method. The experi ental results also verified reasonability of lumped para eter ethod and theoretical analysis in this question. The cooling time could be precisely predicted by theoretical analysis. Whatâ€™s more, the whole battery could be regarded as thermal equilibrium while the variation of battery surface temperature is small enough. This work provides a theoretical basis of battery thermal equilibrium to experiments. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018). Keywords: lithium-ion battery; thermal equilibrium; heat conduction differential equation; lumped parameter method; statistical analysis. 1. Introduction Lithium ion batteri s are widely used in the fields of renewable energy and traditional en rgy, because of the high energy density[1]. Currently, the performance of commercially available lithium-ion batteries, including true capability, rate capability, calendar life, cycle life and cycle efficiency, are sensitivity to the temperature. For * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86 - 020 - 87059478; fax: +86 - 020 - 87057795. E-mail address: songwj@ms.giec.ac.cn 2624 Yang Li et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2623â€“26302 Yang Li / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 example, high temperature can accelerate the losses of lithium-ion and active-material and then result in capacity fading. In contrast, the significant drop of charge and discharge capability in low temperature result from the decline in electrolyte, low Li+ diffusion, reduction in reaction kinetics and the increase in SEI layer impedance. Just as part of the capacity is "frozen" at low temperature[2]. The battery heat generation is affected with current, as well as the temperature. In the study of battery thermal performance, it is critical to maintain the battery in thermal equilibrium before discharge. The battery temperature field will be affected by last test if the resting time between two experiments is not enough. It will impact the experimental repeatability. In the aging test of battery, for example, the same conditions should be controlled at initial charge and discharge of each cycle. The temperature rise caused by charge and discharge should be eliminated by resting. The length of resting time determines whether the battery reaches thermal equilibrium. An extensive literature survey found that the resting time of pouch batteries falls in the range of 0.5 âˆ¼ 12 hours[3-6], thus the value of 1 hour is selected in the present study[4]. Resting time was almost selected by subjective feeling, as few literatures provide an objective analysis of how much resting time is needed. Even though thermal equilibrium was confirmed by thermocouple which was set on battery surface, invariable surface temperature does not mean the entire battery reaches thermal equilibrium. Therefore defect still exist in this method. In this work, the resting time of battery with different current rate and ambient temperature will be obtained. Whatâ€™s more, the convection heat transfer coefficient of battery surface can also be gotten with experiments. 2. Methods 2.1 Battery specifications and experimental apparatus A nominally 20Ah Li-ion pouch battery with LiFePO4 cathode and graphite anode is used in the study. The battery is fresh. Dimensions and mass of the battery are 0.171 Ã— 0.221 Ã— 0.007 m3 and 0.485 kg, respectively. The climatic chamber is BPH-120A. The battery tester is Neware BTS-4008. The date collector is Agilent 34972A. 2.2 Testing approach A Neware BTS-4008 battery tester was used to charge/discharge the battery. The battery performance was characterized under different temperatures and current rates during discharge process. As a symmetrical structure in the thickness direction of Li-ion pouch battery, seven T-type thermal couples were chosen to set on the upper surface of battery which was placed horizontally. The schematic diagram was shown in Fig. 1. The temperature variation of near the tabs area was more significant than that of away from the tabs area during the discharge process. Therefore we put some thermocouples near the tabs [5, 7]. The layout of these thermocouples neglected the tabs location of battery. One reason is that excessive heat generation at the connection between the wires and the tabs of the battery will influence the universality of measurement results due to the arising of contact resistance. In addition, the macro thermal property of the tabs, such as the thermal conductivity, specific heat and convective heat transfer coefficient, will be changed with the involvement of the wires. According to the number of thermocouples, the battery was divided into seven zones, as Fig.1 showed. The details of experimental approach were as follows. (1) The battery was placed in 25â„ƒ climatic chamber and rested 1 hour to reach the thermal equilibrium. Then the battery was charged with C/3 rate to the cut-off voltage 3.65 V and followed with constant voltage charge (3.65 V) until the current reaches C/200 (0.1A). (2) Reset the temperature of the climatic chamber (from 55â„ƒ to -20â„ƒ, temperature step was equal to 15â„ƒ). Before discharge, the battery was rested 1 hour to reach the thermal equilibrium. Then discharged with C/4 to the cut- off voltage 2.7V. Data collector was used for recording voltage and temperature. (3) Changed the temperature in step 2 (from 55â„ƒ to -20â„ƒ, temperature step was equal to 15â„ƒ), and repeated step 1 and 2. (4) Changed the current rate in step 2 (the detail is C/2, 1C, 2C, 3C), and repeated step 1, 2 and 3. In order to ensure the initial SOC was the same in each discharge, the battery would be discharged again to cut- off voltage 2.7V at room temperature and then charged at the same temperature 25 degrees Celsius. Yang Li et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2623â€“2630 2625 Yang Li / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 Fig.1 Schematic diagram of thermocouples distribution on battery top surface. 2.3 Validity of experimental analysis To certify the reliability of the experiment, before analyzing data, the accuracy of thermocouples and climatic chamber should be known. We designed a confirmatory experiment. The thermocouples were putted in climatic chamber. Then we set a certain temperature of climatic chamber. The data of thermocouples would be recorded when the instruments reached stability. Fig. 2 Probability distribution and confidence of experimental error. As it shown in Fig.2, the Gauss Amp function was used to fit the experimental data. It is found that the errors of thermocouples were satisfied with Normal distribution N (Î¼, Ïƒ2). There was an interesting scene. Two peaks appeared in the picture. Both peak 1 and peak 2 are satisfied with Normal distribution N1 (Î¼1, Ïƒ12) and N2 (Î¼2, Ïƒ22), where N1 (-0.014, 0.033292), N1 (0.12053, 0.012032). Comparing peak 1 with 2, we can deduce the difference of Î¼ causing by a fluctuation in the temperature of the climatic chamber. The accuracy of climatic chamber is about Â± 0.12â„ƒ. Considering the difference of Ïƒ and the altitude of peaks, a new phenomenon could be found that the Ïƒ and the altitude of peak away from the origin were smaller than that of counterpart aside. The errors probability of climatic chamber is not even. It might be satisfied with Normal distribution too. But this hypothesis needs further experimental demonstration. For this question, we have already known the accuracy of climatic chamber without further experiment. The number of sample was 18860 and both goodness of fit R2 are very approximate 1, by the way, as a result that the experimental data and fitting result are credible. According to fitting function of Normal distribution, the relationship between the confidence coefficient and confidence interval can be gotten by using MATLAB. The confidence coefficient versus confidence interval was shown in the insert graph of Fig. 2. It showed that the confidence coefficient would beyond 0.99 while the confidence interval was over -0.086 ~ 0.086, i.e. we had sufficient confidence to regard the accuracy of the thermocouple as Â± 0.086â„ƒ. 3. Results and discussion The temperature rise can be defined as Î”T, 2626 Yang Li et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2623â€“26304 Yang Li / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 sT T Tï�„ ï€½ ï€­ (1) where T is the temperature on top surface of battery. Ts is the ambient temperature. Before discharge, the temperature of battery is equal to the surrounding temperature. The battery is in thermal equilibrium. As a result, Î”T can also represent the temperature rise of battery after discharge. 3.1 Calculation of convective heat transfer coefficient In order to eliminate ambiguity, we use Î¸ as a counterpart of Î”T due to Î” has multiple meanings in mathematic. Tï�± ï€½ ï�„ (2) Although the temperature field of battery was non-uniform at the end of the discharged[5], but the temperature field of each zone of battery could be assumed as uniform, because the area of zone was small and the non-uniform temperature of battery was limited. The temperature of thermocouple can be deemed as the temperature of corresponding zone. As the thickness direction z of the pouch battery is very small compared to the long y and wide x directions and the temperature difference of each zone is small, we only consider the heat dissipation in the thickness direction of battery. Although the thermal conductivities of wide direction Î»x and long direction Î»y, in general they are equal, are much larger than the thermal conductivity of thickness direction Î»z[8]. In the present work, we assume spatially homogeneous temperature within each zone of the pouch battery. Consequently, we eliminate the temperature difference in the zone. Nevertheless, the heat capacity and the convective heat transfer coefficient between the battery and ambient in climate chamber were still considered in the energy balance equation. Therefore, a lumped thermal model was applied in each zone and the energy balance equation during the relaxation is written as: dmc hA dt ï�± ï�±ï€­ ï€½ (3) Because of the density of the battery being even, where m is the battery mass, and c is the specific heat, h is the convective heat transfer coefficient, and A is the battery surface area. As four areas in the thickness direction can be omitted in the area calculation and these areas are very small compared to areas of parallel planes. A is equal to 2LÃ—W. Before using the lumped parameter method, the applicability should be tested in the first place. The size of Biot number can be used for applicability of judgement. z hBi ï�¤ ï�¬ ï€½ (4) where h is the convective heat transfer coefficient, Î´ is a half of thickness, and Î»z is the thermal conductivity of thickness direction. In the previous work of G. Vertiz et al[9], the main heat properties of a 14Ah -LiFePO4/graphite-commercial large sizes pouch battery were measured by experiment. They measured the specific heat capacity c of battery with different SOC by using the ARC calorimeter. The mean value of the specific heat capacity c equaled to 1114 JÂ·kg- 1Â·K-1. They also measured the thickness thermal conductivity Î»z with different SOC by adopting the hot plate method. The results showed the value of thermal conductivity Î»z at 0%SOC was 0.235 WÂ·m-1Â·K-1. In another previous literature, we found that the convective heat transfer coefficient of pouch battery in the climatic chamber is 25 WÂ·m- 2Â·K-1[10]. In our work, by the way, the convective heat transfer coefficient of each zone would be gotten in following content. As the Biot number of each zone is supposed approximately to 0.372, it was small enough that we can reasonably use the lumped parameter method in this question. Eq. (3) is an inhomogeneous linear differential equation of first order, the analytical solution of this equation was following: 0exp( / )tï�± ï�± ï�´ï€½ ï€­ (5) where Ï„=mc/hA is the characteristic cooling time, Î¸0 is the temperature at interruption of the discharge current at cut- off voltage, and t is time. According to eq. (5), the least-squares algorithm was used to fit the data which were measured by monitoring the battery temperature evolution after interruption of the discharge current at cut-off voltage. Then the convective heat transfer coefficient between battery and climatic chamber was obtained. Before being fitting, two points should be noticed. Based on the conclusion in section 2.3, total error of the experiment approximated Â± 0.2â„ƒ. The data with a temperature difference greater than 0.5â„ƒ was chosen to be fitted. The reliability of the fitting results was confirmed. Yang Li et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2623â€“2630 2627 Yang Li / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 The mixing heat during the relaxation process could not be neglected, unless the transport properties in battery were sufficiently high[11]. Therefore we should reduce the impact of mixing heat during the relaxation process as far as possible. Considering the accuracy of the thermocouple in the experiment was limited, temperature rise Î¸ should be bigger. Under the same conditions, Î¸ was negative correlation with ambient temperature and positive correlation with current rate. This phenomenon was caused by the change of internal resistance[2]. There was an obvious conflict. Thanks to the battery is power type. The influence of the current rate on the mixing heat was relatively small. Even so, three current rates (C/2 1C 2C 3C) would be contrastively analyzed. In low temperature, the transport properties would be extremely reduced. In consequence, we selected the dates at 25 degrees Celsius to analyze. And battery data at 10 and -5 degrees Celsius were also collected to see their applicability. Fig. 3 The temperature evolutions and fitting curves after the interruption of current. As shown in Fig.3, only C/2 and 3C were shown in the figure because of limited space. But the results of all the data which were chosen would be analyzed. The results were shown in table 1. Table 1. characteristic cooling time Ï„ of each zone at different conditions. Zone 1.1# 1.2# 1.3# 2.1# 2.2# 2.3# 3.2# -5â„ƒ 3C 291.5 333.3 352.1 406.5 409.8 414.9 454.5 10â„ƒ 3C 280.9 330.0 352.1 418.4 418.4 423.7 469.5 25â„ƒ 3C 271.0 327.9 353.4 423.7 420.2 427.4 471.7 -5â„ƒ 2C 337.8 358.4 369.0 408.2 406.5 408.2 436.7 10â„ƒ 2C 348.4 370.4 375.9 431.0 425.5 423.7 456.6 25â„ƒ 2C 313.5 362.3 383.1 442.5 436.7 442.5 476.2 -5â„ƒ 1C 331.1 378.8 416.7 436.7 438.6 444.4 471.7 10â„ƒ 1C 350.9 400.0 431.0 452.5 454.5 456.6 476.2 25â„ƒ 1C 278.6 342.5 392.2 425.5 431.0 444.4 485.4 -5â„ƒ C/2 366.3 398.4 403.2 446.4 452.5 444.4 476.2 10â„ƒ C/2 396.8 434.8 454.5 495.0 487.8 483.1 523.6 25â„ƒ C/2 279.3 366.3 469.5 432.9 434.8 458.7 507.6 2628 Yang Li et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2623â€“26306 Yang Li / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 From the figure 3 and table 1, we found the degree of data fitting was good and the value of characteristic cooling time of each zone with different conditions was close to each other. These illustrated the impact of mixing heat during the relaxation process was not significant and confirmed the rationality of the previous hypothesis. The average value Ï„ of for each zone was presented in table 2. The new h and Bi could be calculated by Ï„, as it was shown in table 2. Table 2. Average value of Ï„, new Biot number and convection heat transfer coefficient of each zone. Zone 1.1# 1.2# 1.3# 2.1# 2.2# 2.3# 3.2# Ï„ 320.5 366.9 396.1 434.9 434.7 439.3 475.5 h (W m-2 K-1) 22.3 19.5 18.0 16.4 16.4 16.3 15.0 Biot number 0.332 0.290 0.269 0.245 0.245 0.242 0.224 According table 2, all the new Biot number were smaller than last used, therefore the assumption was still valid. The difference of h was caused by the placement of the battery in climatic chamber. 3.2 Cooling time of surface temperature field According to the conclusion of section 3.1, it was reasonable to keep assuming there was no heat transfer between each zone. In addition to recording the temperature during charge and discharge, it was also necessary to record during relaxation process until the temperature field became homogeneous in the study of battery temperature characteristics. How long will temperature field become homogeneous? We assume that the temperature rise Î¸ is less than 0.1â„ƒ. The cooling time of each zone could be gotten by using Eq. (6). 0ln( )t ï�±ï�´ ï�± ï€½ (6) Fig. 4 The maximum cooling time of battery with different current and different ambient temperature. The longest cooling time with each current rate and ambient temperature were shown in Fig. 4. Due to the safety of battery discharge at 2C and 3C, no experiment was performed at extreme temperature. Decreasing the temperature and increasing the current would increase the heat production of the battery during the discharge process, results in increasing cooling time. If the threshold of temperature uniformity was changed from 0.1â„ƒ to 0.01â„ƒ, the cooling time would increase by 18.2 minutes. 3.3 Temperature distribution in the direction of thickness Keep the previous assumption in which heat transfer only occurred in the thickness direction and temperature of thickness direction was homogeneous at the time of interruption of the discharge current at cut-off voltage. Consequently, the conduction differential equation of this question was simplified as, Yang Li et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2623â€“2630 2629 Yang Li / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 7 2 2 0, 0a t zt z ï�± ï�± ï�¤ ï‚¶ ï‚¶ ï€½ ï€¾ ï€¼ ï€¼ ï‚¶ ï‚¶ (7) B.C. 0 0, 0t z z ï�±ï‚¶ ï€½ ï€¾ ï€½ ï‚¶ (8) 0,h t z z ï�± ï�¬ ï�± ï�¤ ï‚¶ ï€­ ï€½ ï€¾ ï€½ ï‚¶ (9) I.C. 0 0, 0t zï�± ï�± ï�¤ï€½ ï€½ ï‚£ ï‚£ (10) The partial differential equations were solved by using the method of separation of variables, n 2 n0 n n n n1 2sin( , ) cos( )exp( ) sin cosn zz t Foï�¢ï�± ï�± ï�¢ ï�¢ ï�¢ ï�¢ ï�¢ ï�¤ ï‚¥ ï€½ ï€½ ï€­ ï€« ïƒ¥ (11) n ncosBiï�¢ ï�¢ï€½ (12) 2 atFo ï�¤ ï€½ (13) where z-axis is parallel to the thickness, z = 0 means the midpoint of thickness, z = Î´ means the surface of battery. Î¸0 is temperature at the time of interruption of the discharge current at cut-off voltage. Î²n is the eigenvalue of Eq. (11), and can be solved by eq. (12). Fo is the Fourier number written at Eq. (13) and a = Î»/Ï�c is the thermal diffusivity. The temperature distribution in thickness direction is described as eq. (11), and eq. (14) can be used to describe the level of uneven temperature in thickness direction. ( , ) 1 ( , ) z t t ï�± ï�¸ ï�± ï�¤ ï€½ ï€­ (14) where Î¾ is level of uneven temperature in thickness direction. Itâ€™s obvious that Î¾ is independent of initial temperature Î¸0. Î¾ depends on z, t and Bi, as it was shown in Fig. 5. Fig. 5 The temperature evolution with time in thickness direction after the interruption of current. As observed in Fig. 5, the maximum and minimum Bi number of all zones were picked out. As time increased, Î¾ tended to converge to a certain value and the maximum Î¾ of each Bi occurred at z = 0. In this question, Î¾ was almost a constant while time exceeded 1 minute and the maximum Î¾ of the battery in thickness direction was 17%. When Î¸(Î´, t) was small enough, like 0.1â„ƒ, the surface temperature of battery was homogeneous and the cooling time of each case was much larger than 1 minute. Therefore the temperature difference inner the battery could be neglected. Consequently, it could be considered that the entire battery was also in thermal equilibrium when the surface was homogeneous. 4. Conclusion The cooling time of lithium-ion pouch battery at end of discharge was studied experimentally and theoretically. The temperature rise of battery at the time of interruption of the discharge current at cut-off voltage was related to ambient temperature and current rate. The temperature rise was negative correlation with ambient temperature and 2630 Yang Li et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2623â€“26308 Yang Li / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 positive correlation with current rate. This phenomenon was caused by the change of internal resistance. As a result, the cooling time of each condition was different. The cooling time of each condition could be gotten after experimentally analysis. Nonetheless, the veracity of cooling time was limited to the accuracy of experimental apparatus. Therefore we used experiment to verify theoretical analysis. The cooling time could be precisely predicted by theoretical analysis. The following conclusions are drawn: (1) The battery was divided into 7 areas. And the convection heat transfer coefficient h of each zone was gotten by using lumped parameter method. The difference of h was caused by the placement of the battery in climatic chamber. This experiment also proved the validity of the hypothesis which assumed there was almost no heat dissipation inside the battery except in thickness direction, i.e. heat dissipation in other directions was negligible compared with heat transfer in the thickness direction. (2) According to the above conclusions, the cooling time of battery could be worked out by using lumped parameter method. And the temperature field inner the battery can be solved by analytical solution of the conduction differential equation. It confirmed that the battery could be regarded as thermal equilibrium when the surface temperature tends to be constant. This work provides a theoretical basis of battery thermal equilibrium to experiment. Itâ€™s extremely useful for ensuring the repeatability of experimental results. Acknowledgement The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51477171) for the financial support. Reference [1] Van N R. The rechargeable revolution: A better battery.[J]. Nature, 2014, 507(7490):26-8. [2] Ji Y, Zhang YC, Wang CY. Li-Ion Cell Operation at Low Temperatures. J Electrochem Soc. 2013;160:A636- A49. [3] Wang QJ, Song WL, Wang LN, Song Y, Shi Q, Fan LZ. Electrospun polyimide-based fiber membranes as polymer electrolytes for lithium-ion batteries. Electrochimica Acta. 2014;132:538-44. [4] Klein M, Tong S, Park JW. In-plane nonuniform temperature effects on the performance of a large-format lithium-ion pouch cell. Applied Energy. 2016;165:639-47. [5] Song WJ, Chen MB, Bai FF, Lin SL, Chen YZ, Feng ZP. Non-uniform effect on the thermal/aging performance of Lithium-ion pouch battery. Applied Thermal Engineering. 2018;128:1165-74. [6] Wang CY, Zhang GS, Ge SH, Xu T, Ji Y, Yang XG, et al. Lithium-ion battery structure that self-heats at low temperatures. Nature. 2016;529:515-+. [7] Panchal S, Dincer I, Agelin-Chaab M, Fraser R, Fowler M. Experimental and theoretical investigation of temperature distributions in a prismatic lithium-ion battery. Int J Therm Sci. 2016;99:204-12. [8] Wu B, Li Z, Zhang JB. Thermal Design for the Pouch-Type Large-Format Lithium-Ion Batteries I. Thermo- Electrical Modeling and Origins of Temperature Non-Uniformity. J Electrochem Soc. 2015;162:A181-A91. [9] Veitiz G, Oyarbide M, Macicior H, Miguel O, Cantero I, de Arroiabe PF, et al. Thermal characterization of large size lithium-ion pouch cell based on 1d electro-thermal model. J Power Sources. 2014;272:476-84. [10] Chacko S, Chung YMM. Thermal modelling of Li-ion polymer battery for electric vehicle drive cycles. J Power Sources. 2012;213:296-303. [11] Thomas KE, Newman J. Thermal modeling of porous insertion electrodes. J Electrochem Soc. 2003;150:A176- A92. equently, it could be considered that the entire battery was also in thermal equilibrium when the surface was homogeneous. 4. Conclusion The cooling time of lithium-ion pouch battery at end of discharge was studied experimentally and theoretically. The temperature rise of battery at the time of interruption of the discharge current at cut-off voltage was related to ambient temperature and current rate. The temperature rise was negative correlation with ambient temperature and 2630 Yang Li et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2623â€“26308 Yang Li / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 positive correlation with current rate. This phenomenon was caused by the change of internal resistance. As a result, the cooling time of each condition was different. The cooling time of each condition could be gotten after experimentally analysis. Nonetheless, the veracity of cooling time was limited to the accuracy of experimental apparatus. Therefore we used experiment to verify theoretical analysis. The cooling time could be precisely predicted by theoretical analysis. The following conclusions are drawn: (1) The battery was divided into 7 areas. And the convection heat transfer coefficient h of each zone was gotten by using lumped parameter method. The difference of h was caused by the placement of the battery in climatic chamber. This experiment also proved the validity of the hypothesis which assumed there was almost no heat dissipation inside the battery except in thickness direction, i.e. heat dissipation in other directions was negligible compared with heat transfer in the thickness direction. (2) According to the above conclusions, the cooling time of battery could be worked out by using lumped parameter method. And the temperature field inner the battery can be solved by analytical solution of the conduction differential equation. It confirmed that the battery could be regarded as thermal equilibrium when the surface temperature tends to be constant. This work provides a theoretical basis of battery thermal equilibrium to experiment. Itâ€™s extremely useful for ensuring the repeatability of experimental results. Acknowledgement The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51477171) for the financial support. Reference [1] Van N R. The rechargeable revolution: A better battery.[J]. Nature, 2014, 507(7490):26-8. [2] Ji Y, Zhang YC, Wang CY. Li-Ion Cell Operation at Low Temperatures. J Electrochem Soc. 2013;160:A636- A49. [3] Wang QJ, Song WL, Wang LN, Song Y, Shi Q, Fan LZ. Electrospun polyimide-based fiber membranes as polymer electrolytes for lithium-ion batteries. Electrochimica Acta. 2014;132:538-44. [4] Klein M, Tong S, Park JW. In-plane nonuniform temperature effects on the performance of a large-format lithium-ion pouch cell. Applied Energy. 2016;165:639-47. [5] Song WJ, Chen MB, Bai FF, Lin SL, Chen YZ, Feng ZP. Non-uniform effect on the thermal/aging performance of Lithium-ion pouch battery. Applied Thermal Engineering. 2018;128:1165-74. [6] Wang CY, Zhang GS, Ge SH, Xu T, Ji Y, Yang XG, et al. Lithium-ion battery structure that self-heats at low temperatures. Nature. 2016;529:515-+. [7] Panchal S, Dincer I, Agelin-Chaab M, Fraser R, Fowler M. Experimental and theoretical investigation of temperature distributions in a prismatic lithium-ion battery. Int J Therm Sci. 2016;99:204-12. [8] Wu B, Li Z, Zhang JB. Thermal Design for the Pouch-Type Large-Format Lithium-Ion Batteries I. Thermo- Electrical Modeling and Origins of Temperature Non-Uniformity. J Electrochem Soc. 2015;162:A181-A91. [9] Veitiz G, Oyarbide M, Macicior H, Miguel O, Cantero I, de Arroiabe PF, et al. Thermal characterization of large size lithium-ion pouch cell based on 1d electro-thermal model. J

head.ce:title: Thermal equilibrium characteristic of large-size lithium-ion pouch battery: resting time between charge and discharge

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangzhou Institute of Energy Conversion, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Guangzhou, 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Renewable Energy, Guangzhou, 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Renewable Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New and Renewable Energy Research and Development, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New and Renewable Energy Research and Development

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The temperature rise of lithium-ion battery discharging varies with current and ambient temperature. As a key factor to ensure the repeatability of battery experiments, the time of battery reaches thermal equilibrium after discharging will be figured out in this work by experimental and theoretical analysis. The errors of experimental apparatus was confirmed by an innovatively statistical analysis. The pouch battery being divided to 7 areas, convection heat transfer coefficient of each area was gotten with lumped parameter method. The experimental results also verified reasonability of lumped parameter method and theoretical analysis in this question. The cooling time could be precisely predicted by theoretical analysis. What’s more, the whole battery could be regarded as thermal equilibrium while the variation of battery surface temperature is small enough. This work provides a theoretical basis of battery thermal equilibrium to experiments.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The rechargeable revolution: A better battery.[J]
